metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the concrete proving command for AC3's gitignore check (e.g. `git check-ignore -q .rebar/metrics-snapshots.ndjson` run from the repo root against the actual `.gitignore`, not a tmpdir) and note that the test must operate on the real repo path or invoke `git check-ignore` as a subprocess from the repo root.
evidence: 'verified by the test writing under `.rebar/` and asserting the file is not matched by the repo's `.gitignore`'
No concrete mechanism (e.g. `git check-ignore -q .rebar/metrics-snapshots.ndjson`) is named
Standard unit test practice writes to `tmp_path`, which is outside the repo root, making git check-ignore inapplicable
findings.finding: AC3's gitignore-verification proving command is underspecified and may be a fixture-invalidity defect: unit tests that write to a `tmp_path`/tmpdir will NOT be inspected by `git check-ignore` relative to the repo root, so the test as described cannot actually verify that `.rebar/metrics-snapshots.ndjson` escapes the `.rebar/*` gitignore rule.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC3
scenarios: Test writes to /tmp/pytest-xxx/.rebar/metrics-snapshots.ndjson, runs git check-ignore against that path — git reports 'not tracked' for an unrelated reason, giving a false green that does not prove the real gitignore exemption.
The gitignore negation line is accidentally omitted; the test passes anyway because the tmpdir path is not covered by any gitignore rule.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the vague AC3 verification with: 'CI step (or a dedicated test) runs `git check-ignore --verbose .rebar/metrics-snapshots.ndjson` from the repo root and asserts exit code 1 (file is NOT ignored); this proves the `!`-negation line is present and effective.'

evidence: 2 criterion line(s) bundle multiple deliverables with 'and' (split so each is independently verifiable).
findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define a retirement/pruning policy for the snapshot store (e.g., keep last N records, or records within a rolling window) and document how an operator prunes the file without breaking the reader.
evidence: Plan says 'append-only and additive' with no mention of pruning, rotation, compaction, or a max-record count.
The file is committed to git on every CI run; unbounded growth bloats git history and the working tree file over time.
T9 checklist requires 'There is a RETIRE/cleanup path — it doesn't leak/accumulate.'
findings.finding: The plan defines no retirement or pruning path for the append-only NDJSON store, and does not specify a maximum retention horizon, size cap, or periodic compaction strategy. The file grows one record per CI run indefinitely.
findings.location: Approach — 'append-only NDJSON snapshot'; Files — `.rebar/metrics-snapshots.ndjson — new committed artifact'
scenarios: Long-lived project after 2+ years of daily CI: `.rebar/metrics-snapshots.ndjson` contains tens of thousands of lines; git blame/log becomes slow; PR diffs are noisy; no documented way to prune old records without breaking the committed history.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the concrete type and format of `since`/`until` in the `read_snapshots` signature description (e.g., 'ISO 8601 string' or '`datetime` object') and use a matching example value in AC1.
evidence: 'read_snapshots(since,until) yields in-range records' — no type annotation or example value given
AC1: 'assert rec in read_snapshots(since,until)' — parameter types implicit
findings.finding: The `since` and `until` parameter types accepted by `read_snapshots` are not stated — an executor must independently decide whether to use ISO 8601 strings, `datetime` objects, or epoch floats, and that choice ripples into the test assertions and the AC wording ('assert rec in read_snapshots(since,until)').
findings.location: Files section — `src/rebar/metrics/snapshot.py`; Acceptance Criteria AC1
scenarios: Agent implements since/until as naive datetime objects; test fixtures pass ISO strings; CI fails with a TypeError that is not caught by the AC wording.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Files bullet: '`read_snapshots(since: str, until: str)` — ISO 8601 date strings, inclusive bounds, matching the `ts` field written by `write_snapshot`.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify how write_snapshot handles concurrent CI writes — either document that single-writer-per-run is guaranteed by CI configuration, or add file locking (e.g., fcntl.flock or a lock file) to serialize appends safely.
evidence: 'An append-only NDJSON snapshot' — no mention of file locking or atomic append
No CI concurrency constraint mentioned anywhere in the plan
Malformed-line skip only catches invalid JSON, not semantically merged valid JSON from interleaved writes
findings.finding: The plan does not address concurrent write safety: in CI environments multiple jobs may run simultaneously and attempt to append to the same NDJSON file. Concurrent file appends (without locking or atomic rename) can interleave bytes and produce corrupted lines. While the malformed-line skip handles truncated lines, an interleaved-but-syntactically-valid JSON line could silently produce a record with merged/wrong field values.
findings.location: src/rebar/metrics/snapshot.py — write_snapshot / Approach
scenarios: Two CI jobs run in parallel (e.g., matrix builds) and both append at the same time — lines interleave on some OS/FS combinations
A partially-written line from job A is completed by bytes from job B, forming a syntactically valid but semantically corrupt record
findings.suggested_fix: Use fcntl.flock(f, fcntl.LOCK_EX) around the append write, or document in the plan that CI is configured to run snapshot writes serially (single job writes, others read).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that `write_snapshot` serializes concurrent appends — either (a) acquire `fcntl.flock(LOCK_EX)` on the file before writing (matching the pattern in `alert_store.append`), or (b) state an explicit single-writer-at-a-time invariant (e.g. 'only the CI post-step runner calls this, never in parallel') and enforce it in the AC tests.
evidence: Plan: 'write_snapshot(dict) appends a timestamped record to a committed NDJSON file' — no locking or single-writer constraint stated
alert_store.py lines 51-59: the repo's existing NDJSON-append pattern uses fcntl.flock(LOCK_EX) before each line write to prevent interleaving

AC test covers 'malformed line is skipped without raising' but not concurrent writes — so a torn line from a concurrent append is indistinguishable from a separately malformed line and would be silently dropped
ABSENCE: no mention of fcntl.flock, a lock file, or a single-writer assumption anywhere in the plan
findings.finding: write_snapshot's append has no specified serialization mechanism. Concurrent CI jobs (or parallel rebar invocations) appending to the same NDJSON file simultaneously can produce interleaved or torn lines; the reader's 'skip malformed lines' guard then silently discards them — permanently losing the very data the series is meant to preserve. The codebase already has the established pattern for this exact scenario (alert_store.append uses fcntl.flock(LOCK_EX) before writing each line), but the plan neither mandates flock-serialization nor asserts a single-writer-at-a-time invariant that would make the lack of locking safe.
findings.location: Files section: `src/rebar/metrics/snapshot.py` — write_snapshot description; Approach section: 'Append-only and additive'
scenarios: Two CI jobs triggered by the same push (e.g. a matrix build) both call write_snapshot at the same moment, producing an interleaved line that fails JSON parse and is silently dropped
A developer runs rebar metrics locally while a CI job is writing, corrupting the committed series
findings.suggested_fix: In the write_snapshot spec, add: 'acquire fcntl.flock(LOCK_EX) on the open file before writing the line and release it in a finally block, matching the alert_store.append pattern (rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/alert_store.py:51-59). On platforms without fcntl (Windows), document the no-lock fallback.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a test (and AC) for `read_snapshots` on a non-existent or empty snapshot file — assert it returns an empty iterator without raising, covering the zero-snapshot first-run state the plan narrative describes as `unavailable`.
evidence: Plan Tests: 'write_snapshot then read_snapshots round-trips; a range filter excludes out-of-range records; a malformed line is skipped without raising' — no mention of absent/empty file
Plan Approach: 'until a snapshot exists a metric reports `unavailable`' — this behavior is stated but has no corresponding AC or test
AC1 and AC2 both presuppose a file that has been written to; no AC covers the zero-snapshot state
findings.finding: The test plan has no test for `read_snapshots` when the snapshot file does not yet exist (first CI run) or is empty, leaving the empty-file/absent-file path untested for the new reader.
findings.location: Tests section / Acceptance Criteria
scenarios: First CI run: file does not exist yet — `read_snapshots` may raise FileNotFoundError instead of yielding nothing, breaking the command on first use
File exists but is empty (e.g. created by git checkout but never written): reader must return empty iterator, not raise
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Tests: 'read_snapshots on a nonexistent path yields no records and does not raise'. Add AC: '`read_snapshots` on a missing or empty `.rebar/metrics-snapshots.ndjson` yields an empty sequence rather than raising.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm whether `write_snapshot`'s flock-guarded JSONL append and `read_snapshots`'s malformed-line-skip can delegate to (or extract a shared utility from) the existing `alert_store.append()` and `import_ndjson._iter_records()` patterns, rather than re-implementing them a third time in `snapshot.py`.
evidence: src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/alert_store.py:40-59 — append() does: open('a'), fcntl.flock(LOCK_EX), f.write(json.dumps(record) + '\n'), flock(LOCK_UN) — identical to what write_snapshot must do

src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/alert_store.py:62-81 — is_deduped() iterates JSONL lines with `except Exception: continue` for malformed lines — identical to what read_snapshots's corruption-skip must do
src/rebar/_io/import_ndjson.py:54-75 — _iter_records() warns-and-skips on json.JSONDecodeError — the same pattern the plan's read_snapshots corruption-handling AC requires
Plan Tests section: 'a malformed line is skipped without raising' — this is the third independent re-implementation of the same warn-and-skip pattern
findings.finding: write_snapshot's flock-guarded JSONL-append mechanic and read_snapshots's malformed-line-skip mechanic both re-implement functionality that already exists in the codebase: alert_store.append() (flock + json.dumps + open('a')) and alert_store.is_deduped() / import_ndjson._iter_records() (skip-bad-JSON-line on read). The plan adds a new module without referencing these existing patterns or explaining why they can't be shared.
findings.location: Files section — `src/rebar/metrics/snapshot.py`; Tests section — `a corrupt/truncated NDJSON line is skipped`
scenarios: Implementor writes a fourth copy of flock-guarded JSONL append, missing edge cases (flush before unlock, LOCK_UN in finally) that alert_store already handles.
Divergent malformed-line handling behavior between snapshot.py and alert_store/import_ndjson creates inconsistent failure modes across the codebase.
findings.suggested_fix: Extract a thin `rebar._store.jsonl_store` helper (or reuse `alert_store`'s append pattern by reference) for the flock-guarded append, and use `import_ndjson._iter_records` (or a promoted version of it) for the corruption-tolerant reader.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Acknowledge `alert_store.py`'s flock-guarded NDJSON append+skip-reader as prior art and either extract a shared utility for both callers, or explicitly justify why `snapshot.py` should implement this pattern independently.
evidence: alert_store.py lines 40–59: `fcntl.flock(f.fileno(), fcntl.LOCK_EX)` + `json.dumps(record) + '\n'` append pattern
alert_store.py lines 62–80: per-line `json.loads` with `except Exception: continue` skip-bad-line reader pattern
Plan does not mention alert_store or any existing NDJSON append utility as prior art to consider
ABSENCE: no note in plan explaining why a new module is preferred over extending or extracting a shared utility from the existing pattern
findings.finding: The plan proposes a new flock-guarded NDJSON append function (`write_snapshot`) and a malformed-line-skipping reader (`read_snapshots`) without acknowledging that `alert_store.py` (in `src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/alert_store.py`) already implements the identical pattern: `fcntl.LOCK_EX`-guarded `json.dumps(record) + '\n'` append and a skip-on-exception reader. This is a repackaging of existing prior art; the plan should either reuse the pattern's proven implementation or explicitly justify why a new independent copy is warranted.
findings.location: Files section — `src/rebar/metrics/snapshot.py`; Approach section — 'An append-only NDJSON snapshot … is the lowest-friction durable store'
scenarios: The implementor independently recreates locking/skip logic, diverging from the battle-tested version and creating two maintenance surfaces for the same pattern.
A future bug in the locking or skip logic must be fixed in two places.
findings.suggested_fix: Extract the flock-guarded append and malformed-line-skipping reader into a shared helper (e.g., `src/rebar/_store/jsonl_util.py`) that both `alert_store.py` and `snapshot.py` delegate to, eliminating the duplicate pattern.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify an idempotency strategy for `write_snapshot` (e.g., a unique `run_id` field checked before append, or a deduplication pass in `read_snapshots`) so that re-running CI for the same commit/run does not silently duplicate trend data.
evidence: Plan states `write_snapshot(dict)` 'appends a timestamped record' with no mention of uniqueness enforcement or deduplication.
AC only covers round-trip read-back, range filtering, and malformed-line skipping — no AC for re-entrant/idempotent write behaviour.
ABSENCE: no run-ID or content-hash deduplication guard is specified anywhere in the plan.
findings.finding: write_snapshot is not idempotent: if CI retries or re-runs the snapshot step for the same run (e.g., a flaky pipeline reruns a job), it appends a duplicate record with the same timestamp and metric values. The plan specifies no deduplication key (run ID, content hash, or timestamp-uniqueness check), so the trend series silently accumulates phantom data points.
findings.location: ## Files — `src/rebar/metrics/snapshot.py`; ## Acceptance Criteria (all three ACs omit idempotency)
scenarios: CI pipeline retries a failed step that already wrote a snapshot — a second identical record is appended, inflating the trend series.
A developer runs the snapshot command locally and then CI also runs it for the same commit — two records for the same point in time accumulate.
A partial pipeline re-run (e.g., only the metrics step is re-triggered) produces duplicate snapshots without any warning.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a required `run_id` (e.g., `$CI_JOB_ID` or a content hash of `{timestamp, metrics}`) to each record. In `write_snapshot`, before appending, scan the tail of the file for an existing record with the same `run_id` and skip the write if found. Add an AC: 'calling `write_snapshot` twice with the same `run_id` results in exactly one record in the store'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a note to AGENTS.md (or an ADR) documenting the snapshot store location rationale, the required .gitignore negation, and the append-only contract so future contributors don't inadvertently break persistence.
evidence: No mention of ADR, AGENTS.md update, or design-doc entry anywhere in the plan
The gitignore negation interaction is critical but only documented in the plan prose, not in a persistent project artifact
'WITHOUT this negation line the snapshot file is silently gitignored and the series never persists' — a high-stakes invariant with no durable documentation home
findings.finding: The plan introduces a novel persistence pattern (append-only NDJSON snapshot store under .rebar/, with a mandatory .gitignore negation) but does not plan to document this architectural decision in a persistent artifact (ADR, AGENTS.md, or design doc). Future contributors need to know: (1) why .rebar/ and not reports/, (2) that the .gitignore negation must be maintained for persistence, (3) the append-only contract. Losing this context risks the negation being dropped or the store being moved to reports/.
findings.location: Files section / Approach section
scenarios: A future contributor cleans up .gitignore negations and silently breaks persistence
A contributor moves the store to reports/ not knowing why .rebar/ was chosen
Onboarding confusion about why a data file lives under .rebar/
findings.suggested_fix: Add a short section to AGENTS.md under a 'Metrics Snapshot Store' heading covering: path (.rebar/metrics-snapshots.ndjson), why .rebar/ not reports/, the .gitignore negation requirement, and the append-only read contract.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either name the CLI/command file that will import `read_snapshots` in the Files list (if wiring is in-scope for this ticket), or explicitly defer it and remove the 'the command reads it' phrasing from Approach to avoid implying an unenumerated consumer.
evidence: Search of src/rebar/_cli/ for 'metrics' returns no matches.
Plan names no CLI file in its Files list that imports or calls read_snapshots.
Plan statement: 'The command reads it for trend metrics, and until a snapshot exists a metric reports unavailable.'
findings.finding: The plan's Approach section states 'the command reads it [the snapshot store] for trend metrics', referencing a `rebar metrics` CLI command as a consumer of `read_snapshots`, but no such command file exists in `src/rebar/_cli/` and no file is named in the plan that wires `read_snapshots` into any CLI entrypoint. This prospective consumer is referenced but unenumerated.
findings.location: Approach section: 'The command reads it for trend metrics'
scenarios: An implementor completing this ticket has a fully passing test suite but no wiring from the snapshot reader into any command — the described consumer is missing from the edit set.
A reviewer cannot verify the 'command reads it' claim because no command file is listed in scope.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `src/rebar/metrics/__init__.py` to the Files list (with an explicit note to re-export `write_snapshot`/`read_snapshots`) or document that `snapshot.py` is intentionally a private module not re-exported at the package level.
evidence: src/rebar/metrics/__init__.py exists and exports: REGISTRY, MetricSpec, MetricValue, Unavailable, evaluate, is_authoritative from registry.py.
The plan's Files list contains snapshot.py and test_snapshot.py but not __init__.py.
Consistent with existing pattern, package-level consumers would expect to import from rebar.metrics directly.
findings.finding: `src/rebar/metrics/__init__.py` exists and currently re-exports all `registry.py` public symbols at the package level. The plan does not list `__init__.py` in its Files section, leaving it ambiguous whether `write_snapshot` and `read_snapshots` should be added to the package's public `__all__` — an omission that could leave the new API inconsistently accessible.
findings.location: Files section — `src/rebar/metrics/__init__.py` absent
scenarios: A downstream caller writing `from rebar.metrics import read_snapshots` would get an ImportError because __init__.py is not updated — a surprise given the existing re-export pattern.
The omission is minor if snapshot.py is intentionally a private/internal module, but the plan gives no signal of that intent.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Files: `src/rebar/metrics/__init__.py` — add `write_snapshot`, `read_snapshots` to imports and `__all__`, consistent with the existing re-export of `registry.py` symbols.

findings.finding: The plan asserts '.rebar/ is already tracked in git' (citing `.rebar/criteria_routing.json` as evidence), but the actual `.gitignore` has a blanket `.rebar/*` rule (line 20) that ignores ALL `.rebar/` contents — each tracked file requires a surgical `!.rebar/<file>` negation. There is no `!.rebar/metrics-snapshots.ndjson` negation anywhere, so the new snapshot file would be git-ignored and the trend series would silently never accumulate — the exact failure mode the plan warns about for `reports/`.
findings.location: Files section / Approach section — assertion '`.rebar/` is already tracked in git (e.g. `.rebar/criteria_routing.json`)'; AC3 — 'asserting the file is not matched by the repo's `.gitignore`'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Files section: '`.gitignore` — add `!.rebar/metrics-snapshots.ndjson` negation (mirrors `!.rebar/criteria_routing.json` at line 26; required because `.rebar/*` at line 20 blanket-ignores all `.rebar/` contents).' Also update AC3 to explicitly verify the negation rule is present.

findings.finding: `.gitignore` line 20 reads `.rebar/*`, which gitignores ALL `.rebar/` contents. Only specific paths are surgically un-ignored (e.g., `!.rebar/criteria_routing.json`). The plan never lists `.gitignore` as a file to edit, so `!.rebar/metrics-snapshots.ndjson` is never added — the new snapshot file would be silently ignored by git, directly defeating the plan's stated rationale for storing it under `.rebar/`.
findings.location: Files section / AC3 / .gitignore line 20
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Files section: '- `.gitignore` — add `!.rebar/metrics-snapshots.ndjson` after line 20 (the `.rebar/*` wildcard) to surgically un-ignore the snapshot store, matching the existing pattern for criteria_routing.json and trusted_environments.yaml.'

findings.finding: The .gitignore uses a blanket `.rebar/*` ignore with surgical per-file `!`-negations for each tracked artifact; `.rebar/metrics-snapshots.ndjson` is not in any negation list, so it will be silently gitignored — contradicting the plan's claim that '.rebar/ is already tracked in git (e.g. .rebar/criteria_routing.json)'.
findings.location: Files section / Approach section / AC3 — path `.rebar/metrics-snapshots.ndjson`
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Files section: '`.gitignore` — add `!.rebar/metrics-snapshots.ndjson` after the `.rebar/*` block (following the same surgical-negation pattern as `criteria_routing.json` and `plan_review_moves.json`) so git tracks the snapshot file.' Update AC3 to assert that this negation is present and that `git check-ignore -v .rebar/metrics-snapshots.ndjson` returns no match.

findings.finding: The plan asserts '.rebar/ is already tracked in git (e.g. .rebar/criteria_routing.json), so snapshots persist and sync' — but the actual .gitignore has a blanket `.rebar/*` wildcard on line 20 that ignores ALL .rebar/ contents. Only a small set of surgically named exceptions exist (`!.rebar/trusted_environments.yaml`, `!.rebar/criteria_routing.json`, `!.rebar/plan_review_moves.json`, etc.). `.rebar/metrics-snapshots.ndjson` has no such negation, so it would be gitignored and the trend series would silently never accumulate — the exact failure mode the plan claims to avoid by not using `reports/`.
findings.location: Files section ('`.rebar/metrics-snapshots.ndjson` — new committed artifact') and Approach section ('It lives under the tracked `.rebar/` store so it is committed and synced'); also AC3 ('verified by the test … asserting the file is not matched by the repo's .gitignore')
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the **Files** section: '`.gitignore` — add `!.rebar/metrics-snapshots.ndjson` immediately after line 20's `.rebar/*` rule (the same surgical-negation pattern used for `criteria_routing.json`).' Also update AC3 to assert the negation line is present: `assert '!.rebar/metrics-snapshots.ndjson' in Path('.gitignore').read_text()`.

findings.finding: The plan asserts `.rebar/` is 'already tracked in git' and that placing `.rebar/metrics-snapshots.ndjson` there will make the snapshot series persist and sync — but the actual `.gitignore` has a blanket `.rebar/*` rule (line 20) that ignores ALL `.rebar/` contents, with only 7 specific files/dirs surgically un-ignored via `!`-negations. `.rebar/metrics-snapshots.ndjson` is absent from every negation. The file would be silently git-ignored — the exact failure mode the plan claims to avoid. AC line 3's test asserting the file 'is not matched by the repo's `.gitignore`' would fail under the current configuration.
findings.location: Files section ('.rebar/metrics-snapshots.ndjson'), Approach section ('lives under the tracked `.rebar/` store'), AC line 3 ('asserting the file is not matched by the repo's `.gitignore`')
findings.suggested_fix: In the Files section, add `.gitignore` as a file to modify, with the entry `!.rebar/metrics-snapshots.ndjson` inserted after line 20's `.rebar/*` rule (following the same surgical-negation pattern used for `criteria_routing.json` and `trusted_environments.yaml`). The AC line 3 test wording already implies this check — ensuring it passes requires the negation to exist first.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify and test that `write_snapshot`/`read_snapshots` resolve the snapshot path relative to the configured workspace root (not CWD), consistent with how other `.rebar/` paths are resolved, so the store works correctly in remote-MCP and explicitly-located-workspace deployments.
evidence: Plan states 'a small JSON record appended per CI run' and '.rebar/metrics-snapshots.ndjson — new committed artifact' but never specifies whether the path is resolved relative to CWD, repo root, or a configured workspace path.
The 'Project location and access' matrix cell explicitly includes 'explicitly located workspace, server outside the checkout, no unrestricted-local-filesystem assumption'.

If `snapshot.py` uses a bare relative path like `Path('.rebar/metrics-snapshots.ndjson')`, this resolves to the process CWD, which on a remote MCP server is the server's working directory, not the target repo.
findings.finding: The plan specifies `write_snapshot` appends to `.rebar/metrics-snapshots.ndjson` but does not define how this path is resolved. In deployments where rebar runs as a remote MCP server or against an explicitly located workspace (not the CWD), the path would resolve relative to the server process's CWD, not the target project's `.rebar/` directory, silently writing snapshots to the wrong location and leaving the target project's trend series empty.
findings.location: Files — `src/rebar/metrics/snapshot.py` and `.rebar/metrics-snapshots.ndjson`
scenarios: Remote MCP server shape: rebar server is started in `/opt/rebar/`; `write_snapshot` appends to `/opt/rebar/.rebar/metrics-snapshots.ndjson` instead of the target project's `.rebar/`; the target project's trend series silently never accumulates; no error is raised.

Explicitly located workspace shape: user invokes rebar with `--workspace /projects/myapp`; snapshot is written to CWD's `.rebar/` rather than `/projects/myapp/.rebar/`; git commit in the target repo never sees the new record.
findings.suggested_fix: Pass the workspace root into `write_snapshot`/`read_snapshots` (or derive it from the same workspace-resolution utility used elsewhere in rebar), rather than using a bare relative path. Add an AC verifying the path resolves to `<workspace_root>/.rebar/metrics-snapshots.ndjson`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the concurrency/locking policy for `write_snapshot` (e.g., 'single-writer assumed — CI workflow serializes the snapshot step' OR 'use `fcntl.flock` for append safety') so the executor does not have to choose.
evidence: 'write_snapshot(dict) appends a timestamped record' — no locking or atomicity constraint stated
'appended per CI run' — concurrent CI runs are common in many repos
findings.finding: The plan does not state the expected behavior when two CI runs append to the file concurrently — no file-locking or atomic-append strategy is mentioned, leaving the executor to choose (or silently produce a corrupt NDJSON file under parallel CI).
findings.location: Files section — `src/rebar/metrics/snapshot.py`; Approach section
scenarios: Two parallel CI matrix jobs both call write_snapshot simultaneously; one write partially overwrites the other's line, producing a malformed NDJSON entry that is silently skipped by the reader, losing data.
findings.suggested_fix: Add one sentence to the Approach or Files sections: 'Concurrent writes are out of scope — the CI workflow MUST serialize the snapshot step (e.g., a single post-job); `write_snapshot` does not implement file locking.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify (and test) write_snapshot's error-handling contract: at minimum, log a warning and re-raise (or surface via return value) on I/O failure so operators can detect accumulation failures.
evidence: No error-handling specification for write_snapshot anywhere in the plan
'until a snapshot exists a metric reports unavailable' — implies silent accumulation failure is catastrophic to the feature's purpose
Tests section covers read-side malformed-line skip but no write-side failure test
findings.finding: write_snapshot has no specified error-handling behavior: if the append fails (disk full, permissions error, I/O error), the plan is silent on whether the error is logged, surfaced, or swallowed. A silent write failure means the metric series silently stops accumulating — the exact problem the plan exists to prevent.
findings.location: src/rebar/metrics/snapshot.py — write_snapshot / Acceptance Criteria
scenarios: CI runner has a permissions issue on .rebar/ — writes silently fail, metrics never accumulate
Disk-full condition on CI agent — write fails, no log, operator has no signal
findings.suggested_fix: In write_snapshot, wrap the append in a try/except IOError, log a structured warning with the error and snapshot data, then re-raise so the CI step can fail visibly rather than silently.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the write-concurrency model for `write_snapshot` — either restrict to single-writer (document that only one CI step calls it) or specify an advisory file-lock / atomic-rename approach — and add a test or AC covering concurrent-write safety.
evidence: Plan says 'appended per CI run' without restricting to single-writer CI topology.
No lock, atomic rename, or advisory file-lock mechanism is mentioned in the plan or tests.

On Windows, concurrent O_APPEND writes to the same file from separate processes are not atomic; on Linux they are only atomic below PIPE_BUF (4096 bytes), which a JSON line with many metrics fields could exceed.
The AC for corruption tolerance treats corruption as an external hazard, but the plan's own write path can produce it under parallelism.
findings.finding: The plan does not address concurrent writes from parallel CI jobs. File append in a multi-process scenario (e.g., parallel matrix jobs both running `write_snapshot`) is not atomically safe beyond PIPE_BUF on Linux and is not atomic at all on Windows, meaning two concurrent appenders can interleave partial JSON lines, producing corrupt NDJSON records. The plan only says lines are 'skipped' on corruption but does not prevent it.
findings.location: Approach — 'An append-only NDJSON snapshot … appended per CI run'; AC — 'a corrupt/truncated NDJSON line is skipped'
scenarios: CI matrix with parallel jobs (e.g., coverage job and complexity job running simultaneously): both call `write_snapshot`; their append syscalls interleave; one or more NDJSON lines are split mid-record; the reader silently skips those records; trend data is lost without any error surfaced to the operator.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
